questions.q: The Pandyas ruled the far south of India from which great city?
questions.explanation: Madurai was the principal capital of the Pandya dynasty.

questions.q: The Pandyas were one of the three ancient kingdoms of which region?
questions.explanation: The Pandyas were one of the three crowned Tamil dynasties, with the Cheras and Cholas.

questions.q: The Pandyas were especially renowned for the fishery of which precious natural product?
questions.explanation: The Pandya realm was famous for its pearl fishery in the Gulf of Mannar.

questions.q: The royal emblem of the Pandya dynasty was which symbol?
questions.explanation: The Pandya emblem was the fish (often a twin-fish), distinct from the Chola tiger and Chera bow.

questions.q: The Pandyas patronised poetry during which famous early Tamil literary age?
questions.explanation: The Pandyas were noted patrons of Sangam-era Tamil poetry, traditionally hosting the academies (Sangams) at Madurai.

questions.q: Madurai, the Pandya capital, is located in which present-day Indian state?
questions.explanation: Madurai lies in present-day Tamil Nadu.

questions.q: The famous temple in the Pandya capital dedicated to the goddess Meenakshi is located in which city?
questions.explanation: The Meenakshi Amman temple stands in Madurai, long the Pandya seat.

questions.q: The Pandyas had two great phases of power: the early Sangam age and a later revival in which broad period?
questions.explanation: The Pandyas revived strongly in the medieval period, especially the 13th century CE.

questions.q: The Pandyas were principally what kind of power?
options: A Tamil dynasty of the deep south
questions.explanation: The Pandyas were one of the ancient Tamil dynasties of the southern peninsula.

questions.q: Pandya pearls and trade connected their kingdom with merchants from which distant western power, among others?
questions.explanation: Pandya pearls and goods reached Roman and other foreign markets through busy southern ports.

questions.q: Which Pandya king is celebrated in Sangam poetry for victory at the Battle of Talaiyalanganam over a confederacy of rivals?
questions.explanation: The Pandya Nedunjeliyan is praised in Sangam works for his triumph at Talaiyalanganam.

questions.q: Which ruler is traditionally regarded as the founder of the medieval (later) Pandya revival in the 6th-7th centuries?
questions.explanation: Kadungon is credited with reviving Pandya power after the Kalabhra interregnum, founding the later line.

questions.q: The Venetian traveller Marco Polo visited and described the wealth of which south Indian kingdom in the late 13th century?
questions.explanation: Marco Polo described the prosperity and pearl trade of the Pandya kingdom (which he called Maabar).

questions.q: Which medieval Pandya king greatly expanded the kingdom in the 13th century and led the so-called 'golden age' of Pandya power?
questions.explanation: Jatavarman Sundara Pandya I led aggressive conquests in the mid-13th century, marking the Pandya zenith.

questions.q: The Pandyas frequently warred against which neighbouring Tamil empire that often dominated them in the 9th-12th centuries?
questions.explanation: The Cholas frequently subdued and absorbed the Pandya country during their height.

questions.q: The pearl fishery that enriched the Pandyas was located in which body of water?
options: The Bay of Bengal off Kolkata
questions.explanation: The Gulf of Mannar between southern India and Sri Lanka was the famed Pandya pearl-fishing ground.

questions.q: Which Delhi Sultanate general raided the Pandya country and sacked Madurai in the early 14th century?
questions.explanation: Malik Kafur, general of Alauddin Khalji, raided the Pandya country and plundered Madurai around 1311.

questions.q: After Malik Kafur's invasions, a breakaway Muslim state formed in the former Pandya region; it was known as which sultanate?
questions.explanation: The short-lived Madurai (Ma'bar) Sultanate arose in the early 14th century in the old Pandya country.

questions.q: The three crowned kings of the Tamil land, the muvendar, were the Cheras, Cholas, and which third dynasty?
questions.explanation: The muvendar (three crowned kings) were the Cheras, Cholas, and Pandyas.

questions.q: The later Pandyas often had multiple kings ruling at once under a system described as what?
options: Co-regency / collateral rule by several Pandya princes
questions.explanation: The later Pandyas frequently practised collateral or joint rule, with several princes holding royal titles simultaneously.

questions.q: Which interregnum group is traditionally said to have eclipsed the early Tamil dynasties, including the Pandyas, before Kadungon's revival?
questions.explanation: The Kalabhra interregnum is said to have disrupted the early Tamil dynasties before the Pandya revival under Kadungon.

questions.q: The Pandya king Maravarman Sundara Pandya I notably defeated and ended the dominance of which weakening dynasty in the early 13th century?
questions.explanation: Maravarman Sundara Pandya I struck decisive blows against the declining Cholas, reasserting Pandya supremacy.

questions.q: Jatavarman Sundara Pandya I is recorded as performing a ceremony of 'covering with gold' at which famous temple?
questions.explanation: Jatavarman Sundara Pandya I is famed for gilding the hall and shrine at the Chidambaram temple.

questions.q: A succession dispute between two Pandya princes, Vira Pandya and Sundara Pandya, in the late 13th century invited intervention that hastened the dynasty's fall; this civil war preceded which event?
options: The fall of Vijayanagara at Talikota
questions.explanation: The Pandya civil war between Vira and Sundara Pandya weakened the realm just before Malik Kafur's invasions.

questions.q: Megasthenes and classical sources record a tradition that the Pandya kingdom was founded or named after a woman; the Greeks linked it to a daughter of which hero?
questions.explanation: Greek accounts via Megasthenes connected the Pandya land to 'Pandaia', described as a daughter of the Indian Herakles.

questions.q: An embassy from a Pandya king is recorded as reaching the court of which Roman emperor around the start of the Common Era?
questions.explanation: Roman sources note a south Indian (Pandya) embassy reaching Emperor Augustus, reflecting active trade contact.

questions.q: The medieval Pandyas were eventually overrun and absorbed in the 14th-16th centuries by the expansion of which southern empire and its Nayaks?
questions.explanation: After the sultanate interlude, Vijayanagara and its Madurai Nayaks absorbed the former Pandya country.

questions.q: The Sinnamanur and Velvikudi copper-plate inscriptions are key sources for the genealogy of which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Velvikudi and Sinnamanur plates provide important genealogical records of the later Pandya line.

questions.q: The Pandya king Maravarman Rajasimha and his successors fought a prolonged contest in the 8th-9th centuries with which northern Tamil-region dynasty over the central Tamil land?
questions.explanation: The earlier medieval Pandyas waged long wars with the Pallavas of Kanchi for control of the Tamil heartland.

questions.q: Marco Polo's account of the Pandya land notes the local custom of diving for pearls and refers to the region by which name?
questions.explanation: Marco Polo called the Pandya coast 'Maabar' and described its pearl diving and great wealth.
